data.title: Stan Druckenmiller Says 'We Don't Know How This Movie Is Going to Play Out' on AI, Jobs and Inflation — Here's How Workers Can Prepare for What's Next
data.description:
Quick Summary

A lot of people are treating artificial intelligence (AI) like it will slash jobs, crush wages and push prices down. 

Stan Druckenmiller wants you to slow down before you buy that script.

"I don't think any of us know how this movie is going to play out," the billionaire investor said in a recent conversation with Morgan Stanley's Iliana Bouzali, pushing back on the idea that AI will definitely be deflationary and trigger "massive job losses."

His main objection is that people are drawing hard conclusions from something that's still unfolding. "Anybody who believes that with conviction suffers from arrogance and not an open mind," he said.

Why he thinks the jobs story is more complicated

Druckenmiller, who is famously known for achieving roughly 30% annual returns every year for 30 years, said that every big wave of technology has been greeted with predictions of mass unemployment, "all the way back to the horse and buggy." 

It hasn't worked out that way, though. Jobs have changed, but they haven't disappeared. That's why it is always good to stay prepared by speaking with a financial advisor. ​

He pointed to an example from his own life. Years ago, after seeing early medical AI that could read scans as well as human experts, he told a radiologist in his family they might be out of work in five or ten years. The software was that good. 

"We have more radiologists now than we had 10 years ago," he said, alluding to the fact that the work has shifted instead of vanishing.

Machines handle more of the pattern recognition on scans. Radiologists spend more time talking with patients, explaining what the images mean and walking through decisions. "He has more time to do the real stuff," Druckenmiller said. 

AI, inflation and the risk of getting the story wrong

On prices, Druckenmiller calls AI "obviously" disinflationary at first glance. Smarter software, more automation, fewer hours for the same output—that sounds like downward pressure on costs.

But he immediately points to how quickly macro stories can flip. When COVID hit, he noted, five‑year forward rates collapsed, and plenty of smart people expected a long stretch of low inflation. Five years later, inflation was 9%.

Even if the pessimists about AI and jobs are right, the second‑order effects could look very different from what people are assuming. If policymakers respond to job losses with aggressive money printing or some form of universal income, you could end up with an inflation problem that comes from the policy response, not the technology itself. 

"After all, inflation is caused by money," he reminded.​

What this means for your own plan

Druckenmiller's advice to professionals is to stay mentally prepared for outcomes the crowd might be missing so you can adjust quickly when reality starts to show its hand. That's useful guidance for household finances, too.

That's where outside help can matter, and SmartAsset can help by connecting you with financial advisors based on your location, asset level and goals. 

Instead of coldcalling firms or scrolling through endless bios, answer this short questionnaire and SmartAsset's free matching tool pairs you with up to three advisors who actively work with clients like you. 

You can't control how the AI movie plays out, what you can do is build a financial plan that assumes the plot may twist and have a professional in your corner to help you adapt when it does.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: $100 Invested In McDonald's 20 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) has outperformed the market over the past 20 years by 3.33% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 11.83%. Currently, McDonald's has a market capitalization of $233.09 billion.

Buying $100 In MCD: If an investor had bought $100 of MCD stock 20 years ago, it would be worth $939.99 today based on a price of $326.25 for MCD at the time of writing.

McDonald's's Performance Over Last 20 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.
